records.ety: [OE. desdain, disdein, OF. desdein, desdaing, F. ddain, fr. the verb. See Disdain, v. t.]

defs: A feeling of contempt and aversion; the regarding anything as unworthy of or beneath one; scorn.
quotes: How my soul is moved with just disdain!
Disdain and scorn ride sparkling in her eyes.
notes: Often implying an idea of haughtiness.

defs: That which is worthy to be disdained or regarded with contempt and aversion.
quotes: Most loathsome, filthy, foul, and full of vile disdain.

defs: The state of being despised; shame.

records.ety: [OE. disdainen, desdainen, OF. desdeigner, desdaigner, F. ddaigner; des- (L. dis-) + daigner to deign, fr. L. dignari to deem worthy. See Deign.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Disdained (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Disdaining.]

defs: To think unworthy; to deem unsuitable or unbecoming; as, to disdain to do a mean act.
quotes: Disdaining . . . that any should bear the armor of the best knight living.

defs: To reject as unworthy of one's self, or as not deserving one's notice; to look with scorn upon; to scorn, as base acts, character, etc.
quotes: When the Philistine . . . saw David, he disdained him; for he was but a youth.
'T is great, 't is manly to disdain disguise.

defs: To be filled with scorn; to feel contemptuous anger; to be haughty.
quotes: And when the chief priests and scribes saw the marvels that he did . . . they disdained.
